description:
You are a system administrator who has just received the raw daily status data for all production servers. Your goals are to organise the data under /home/user/server_reports, validate it against a JSON-Schema, extract useful subsets with jq and produce a very small human-readable summary. OBJECTIVE When you finish, the following four artefacts must exist and have exactly the contents/format described below (all paths are absolute). The automated grader will open and inspect each of them byte-for-byte, so pay close attention to spacing, new-lines, capitalisation, key order, etc. 1. /home/user/server_reports/server-status-2024-05-14.json • A JSON array containing five server objects in the exact order shown here. • Every object must have four keys in the exact order id, uptime, load, status. • Numeric values must not be quoted. All indents are two spaces. 
[
  {
    "id": "web-01",
    "uptime": 876,
    "load": 0.45,
    "status": "OK"
  },
  {
    "id": "web-02",
    "uptime": 43,
    "load": 2.90,
    "status": "CRIT"
  },
  {
    "id": "db-01",
    "uptime": 1290,
    "load": 1.20,
    "status": "OK"
  },
  {
    "id": "cache-01",
    "uptime": 300,
    "load": 0.80,
    "status": "WARN"
  },
  {
    "id": "worker-01",
    "uptime": 210,
    "load": 3.10,
    "status": "CRIT"
  }
]
2. /home/user/server_reports/server-status.schema.json • A Draft-07 JSON-Schema that exactly describes the structure of server-status-2024-05-14.json. • The schema must forbid additional properties. 
{
  "$schema": "http://json-schema.org/draft-07/schema#",
  "type": "array",
  "items": {
    "type": "object",
    "required": ["id", "uptime", "load", "status"],
    "properties": {
      "id":     { "type": "string" },
      "uptime": { "type": "integer", "minimum": 0 },
      "load":   { "type": "number",  "minimum": 0 },
      "status": { "type": "string",  "enum": ["OK", "WARN", "CRIT"] }
    },
    "additionalProperties": false
  }
}
3. /home/user/server_reports/critical-servers-2024-05-14.json • A JSON array, produced with jq, that contains *only* the two objects whose status equals "CRIT". • The objects must appear in the same order they were in the source file and use the identical formatting/indentation. 
[
  {
    "id": "web-02",
    "uptime": 43,
    "load": 2.90,
    "status": "CRIT"
  },
  {
    "id": "worker-01",
    "uptime": 210,
    "load": 3.10,
    "status": "CRIT"
  }
]
4. /home/user/server_reports/summary-2024-05-14.txt • A single-line, semi-colon-separated summary of server health. • Exactly this content, no leading/trailing spaces, followed by a terminating newline: 
TOTAL_SERVERS=5; OK=2; WARN=1; CRIT=2
LOGGING REQUIREMENT Create a log file called /home/user/server_reports/validation.log. After you perform JSON-Schema validation, append exactly one of the following two lines (and nothing else) to this file: • VALIDATION PASSED • VALIDATION FAILED The test harness will only accept the task if the line reads “VALIDATION PASSED”. CONSTRAINTS & HINTS • Use only standard command-line tools available in a minimal Linux environment (e.g. mkdir, echo, cat, jq, python3). • If you rely on Python for validation, write a short script alongside your data and call the builtin json and jsonschema libraries. If the latter is missing, consider using pip --user install jsonschema. Whatever approach you choose, remember to capture only the single PASS/FAIL line inside validation.log. • All four output files must already exist and contain the exact texts above when you finish; the grader will not execute your commands, only check the filesystem contents. • Keep your command count sensible (roughly 5-10 separate commands is typical for this workflow). The task is complete when every file and directory enumerated above exists with the exact contents specified and the file validation.log contains the string “VALIDATION PASSED”.
name: task_000000_45727d3b
